column17: While I haven't officially done a whole lot of competitive programming I was extremely involved in competition math during high school, and from the experience I do have with competitive programming, the basic ideas are the same: you get to know the subject(s), and you gain a level of understanding of these topics that is so fundamental and so basic that (and this is the coolest part) you start to see connections between them and other things that you never would have previously thought to ever be true, and you start to see them pop up, almost obviously like a gut feeling, in what might have before seemed like the strangest places --- i.e. some of those super-random seeming competition problems. I just love the feeling of seeing and making those connections and taking something that seems so complicated and strange and following it through to those clean, awesome solutions that competition problems often tend to have (dirtier/more complicated solutions are cool too; it just feels great to solve these problems in general)
column18:

also some bonuses -- Putnam 2003 A5, with a very nice bijection

column17: Frankly, I joined ACM without any direction or leaning. However, within a day of signing up for the membership, I found myself watching the Instagram takeover of ACM and was captivated by Nikil's interests and his contributions to ICPC. Soon, the advanced training workshop fully expanded my horizons on what the definition of "computing" is: it is an eye-widening one-second moment that comes from hours of practice. I was told to adopt a growth mindset in my LS7A (intro to cell biology) class, and that is to persistently seek discomfort and be eager to reach for more practice. It seems to me that joining the ICPC committee would train me in the art of staying calm in time-sensitive scenarios, to exercise logical interpreting in group settings, and essentially *throw* me a dictionary of new computing vocabulary. Fortunately, I am equally a good thrower and a good receiver--- I have taught middle-school students the basics of machine learning (what is machine learning? What is AI?) and robotics (using arduino). The teaching component of ICPC would fit perfectly with my experience, even though I am a freshman.
column18: My favorite problem would have to come from the electron emission spectrum or atomic emission spectra. It is a direct visual representation of the electron's movement! It literally shows the electron jumping from one level to another and by knowing if it's a Lyman series or Balmer series you would know its starting level and thereby compute its ending level. I really enjoy figuring out a problem by knowing exact steps to approach the problem, and even more if I can see it (well, technically I haven't ever seen a single electron moving, but I can certainly draw a diagram of it)!

column18:
My favorite problem is a whack-a-mole question that goes along the lines of:

You are playing a whack-a-mole game with a 1D row of mole holes, which can either have a mole up in it or not. This is represented by an array of boolean values. You hit them with a hammer of width n, capable of hitting n moles in a row. Write a program that returns the maximum number of moles that you can knock with a single hit of the hammer.

It's my favorite because I remember the excitement of when I was able to figure out the O(N) solution quickly, which I rarely had done before. (My answer was to make a parallel array that would track the number of moles you would hit if that index were the leftmost index of the hammer, using a linear scan through the mole array. Then, you can find the maximum value of the second array.) I love the feeling of solving a challenge, and I remembered this one vividly.

column18:
This is one of the most interesting problems I encountered when I was in high school:

Q: You are on a small boat, isolated in the middle of the ocean. The only way you can save yourself is by throwing two flares in the sky exactly 45 minutes apart. You only have two flares (which cannot be used for anything other than throwing), so you want to be smart about how you use them. To help you measure time, you have a lighter and two irregularly shaped ropes which you may burn. You know that each rope takes exactly 60 minutes to completely burn. Assuming that the time taken to light the ropes/flares and throwing them does not count, how will you time the 45 minutes? (There is no way to know how much time has elapsed by looking at the ropes since they are completely irregular and assume that after spending so many days out in the ocean, your sense of time has degraded from bad to extremely poor).

Solution (Spoiler):
Light the first rope from both ends and the second rope from one end. At this time throw the first flare into the sky.
When the first rope completely finishes burning, you have completed 30 minutes. At this point, immediately light the other end of the second rope. The second rope must have burned half its time (30 minutes) already and now burning from both ends will completely burn out in another 15 minutes. At this point in time, you have successfully timed 45 minutes, so toss the second flare into the sky!

My favorite problem is a follows: Is there a way to map every value in the real numbers to the natural numbers? I personally love this problem because of a personal experience. Understanding why this mapping doesn't exist and what that said about infinity had always baffled me until I was reading James Munkres's "Topology" that it all made sense to me. 

This is because the proof of the mapping's nonexistence relied on the properties of the two spaces. The fact that for any two real numbers there exists a number that is in between these two numbers makes the mapping impossible. You could just choose a new number that was in between the points f(n) and f(n+1).

This taught me that the solution to problem solving is to never do more work than one has to. Find the exact minimum properties needed and only prove those. This valuable insight has greatly helped me both in math and life. But I hope to gain many more from ICPC

column17:
Since the days of hunting for Easter eggs as an infant, I’ve been competitive out of the womb. Throughout school, I always found myself involved with competitions, whether it be Math Olympiad, hackathons, or even USACO. When it comes to training for events throughout the year, ICPC appeals heavily to the side of me that’s always looking for a competition.
On top of just competing, I’ve also had an appreciation for the beauty of algorithms. Specifically, seeing a cleanly presented solution to a problem is always extremely satisfying to me, and I’m inclined to learn that formula. Since I’m currently not taking a computer science class, ICPC provides me with my fair share of algorithms.
Of course, I wouldn’t have any of the knowledge I currently have (both in CS and beyond) if it weren’t for amazing teachers and mentors. Thus, I’m always looking for ways to pay it forward by teaching myself. Seeing the ICPC officers dedicate their time each week towards teaching a range of skill levels makes me want to continue that legacy and mentor newer students getting into competitive programming or just CS as a whole.
column18: My favorite problem would have to be printing out the nth Fibonacci number. This problem seemed so simple to me when I was just starting out CS; just write a recursive function and you’re done right? Wrong! After seeing the stack overflow because of too many recursive calls, I was taught to store intermediate values along the way, which instantly streamlined the solution. Yet even then, another solution would be to calculate from bottom-up, saving memory from stored intermediate values. I was reminded of the subtle complexities of this problem when recently exposed to the similar problem of distinct ways to climb n steps at the ICPC Coding Interview Crash Course. This prompted me to continue thinking about what type of problems I could reduce down to a sequence as such, hopefully netting me a solution or two in future interviews.
column19: Starting freshman year of high school, I began teaching Java for Silicon Valley Youth, a local non-profit. While students came in at varying levels, I was able to gear the class specifically towards each student by providing both easy and challenge problems. Through balancing class concepts, I made sure that by the end of my class, students would leave as competent coders.

column18: My favorite problem is actually quite ancient. The story is in ancient Greece there was a puzzle: how large is the Earth? Archimedes knew that 50 miles away in another city, there is no shadow right at noon. By measuring the length of shadow in his own city, and given the distance between the two cities, he was able to estimate the radius of Earth. The geometry behind is not complicated at all, but I still think the method is really cool. A related problem is to estimate the radius of Earth only from a photo of sunset.

column18: I am enthralled by invariants, and any problems that involve them. The most obvious examples are the problems where numbers are erased and replaced on a blackboard. If you were to replace two numbers on a blackboard with the GCD and LCM, their product would be an invariant, and the sum would never decrease. Generally, invariants are such a nice way of looking at what could otherwise be very challenging problems, and result in elegant and efficient solutions. For example, the sum of the digits is invariant when taking mod 9, so any problems involving S(n) can be taken modulo 9 and made much easier. Invariants sum up what I love most about problem solving and computer science. They are examples of the number of different ways in which seemingly ugly problems can be tackled with beautiful and efficient solutions, often with just a little ingenuity or creativity.

I'd say I was always intrigued by the Monty Hall problem. Calculus is my favorite branch of math and I haven't really paid much attention to probability but this problem is very interesting to me. It goes this way, "Suppose you're on a game show, and you're given the choice of three doors: Behind one door is a car; behind the others, goats. You pick a door, say No. 1, and the host, who knows what's behind the doors, opens another door, say No. 3, which has a goat. He then says to you, "Do you want to pick door No. 2?" Is it to your advantage to switch your choice? " (from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monty_Hall_problem). Even though one might say it shouldn't matter since every door has 1/3 possibility of having the car, one of the simple solutions is "A player who stays with the initial choice wins in only one out of three of these equally likely possibilities, while a player who switches wins in two out of three."

No matter how many times I read the problem and the many proposed solutions, I always somehow go into this infinite loop of thinking about different cases and probabilities. I then realize how complex one simple question could be.

One of my favourite problems is the quantum delayed choice experiment. It's really interesting because, as most unsolved problems in the world of quantum mechanics do, it could have really wacky implications about the nature of space and time1

One worth mentioning that is competitive programming related, though, is a three part problem on Codeforces, I believe called the “Great Julya Calendar”. It’s really straight-forward to solve for input of size 10^6, but requires a few cool optimizations and tricks to get it to work for larger inputs, like 10^12 and 10^18. I’m sure these type of problems are common, but for me, it succinctly and for the first time illustrated both how dramatically problem difficulty can be affected by the size of the input, and in attempting to solve it and looking at the solution, how the same information can be acquired and conveyed in a variety of inventive ways. I really enjoyed and learned a lot from it!

column18: I’d say my favorite problem, or at least the one I’ve spent the most time thinking about, is how to win minimax. Minimax is a game where you have some tree with an arbitrary depth and an arbitrary number of branches at each node. Every node at the bottom has some value assigned to it. There are two players, each taking a turn by choosing a branch down the game tree to go. The goal of one player is to reach the highest value they can, and the goal of the other player is to reach the lowest value they can.

column18: While I am still exploring all the algorithm problems that CS has to offer, the one that I first heard about that intrigued me is the open P vs. NP problem. I'm not sure if this counts as a problem, per say, since it is still an unsolved problem, but it's extremely intriguing. In essence, the question is whether a problem whose solution is quickly verifiable can also be solved quickly. While P problems can be solved and verified quickly, NP problems can be verified quickly, but the problems have an unknown difficulty. To be in set P means that it runs in polynomial time. NP, which stands for nondeterministic polynomial time, is not immediately assumed to be more "difficult". Rather, there is no known polynomial time solution to these problems, if it exists at all. The problem is whether P = NP or P != NP. While problems in P must be in NP, the issue is whether problems in NP can lie in P. This problem has existed for nearly 50 years, and a possible solution to this would have massive effects on multiple fields, including cryptography, game theory, AI, mathematics, and many more.

column14:
I’m a first-year so I haven’t been a part of any UCLA organizations prior to this quarter.

In the past, I’ve been Editor-in-Chief of my high school newspaper - The Howler, Co-Founder/Co-Marketing Director for Irvine Hacks, and Founder/President of Tectify.

I think The Howler was a super transformative experience, as going into high school I was very STEM-oriented and honestly didn’t really pay attention too much to the news or the world around me. Holding discussions over topics from the 2nd Amendment in light of devastating campus shootings to the digital divide and educational equity, I opened my eyes to the complexities of global issues and increased my empathy with others of different views. I’ve become an engaged leader who cares about global issues and strives to impact society positively. As Editor-in-Chief, I wanted to more than simply oversee the paper and strove to revamp our paper for our digital age and led the implementation of Zappar, an app using scannable codes to display augmented reality content. It was super exciting to see students gasp as content popped out onto their phones and it was really fulfilling to have made that lasting impact on the future of our paper.

For Tectify, it was a lot of self motivation and a desire to satisfy the growing demand for technology education in my community beyond school. I really had to get out of my comfort zone in contacting companies and making connections in order to coordinate events. Nonetheless it was super rewarding to have made connections with engineers at companies like Google and Acorns and to see all the bubbling enthusiasm of students at every event.

As Co-Marketing Director of Irvine Hacks, I taught myself graphic design to create our logos and lead the branding for the entire event. It was a super new experience just taking on a bunch of roles such as finding sponsorships, finding a venue, and driving social media presence while working alongside a team working to set up the logistics, plan workshops, and ensure the entire event would run as smoothly as possible. Though we had to do quick problem solving more than once, such as when our initial venue dropped on us last minute, forcing us to reschedule and send dozens of emails to find a new venue, it was totally worth it seeing the large group of attendees our marketing was able to acquire, as well as the prizes we were able to offer as a result of our rigorous sponsorship search. It was also extremely inspiring to see all the innovative projects that students were able to come up with!

column18:
My favorite problem is Stone Game (https://leetcode.com/problems/stone-game/). Warning: spoilers ahead!

I love it because it flies in the face of typical dynamic programming. Sure, you can create a recursive solution, memoize it, then settle on a quadratic-time tabulated solution. However, it turns out that the answer is always the same, and that the optimal solution is simply `return true`.

column17:
For much of my childhood, I saw mathematics and computer science as largely mutually exclusive, and didn’t bother to learn anything about programming. When a friend suggested I check out Project Euler, I picked up a little Python to solve the first few problems and was immediately hooked.

I spent a genuinely unhealthy amount of my free time in 12th grade just solving Project Euler problems and learning to optimise my solutions. I was a little eccentric about not using any libraries to optimise my code and, to a large degree, writing up everything I needed to solve a problem from scratch, and so my favourite thing was to improve upon my algorithm for generating prime numbers. While my progress (in terms of problems solved) was slow — for each hour I spent writing a working solution, I spent five more optimising it and trying to get every last drop of performance possible — I developed a solid foundation in programming I didn’t then think would lead anywhere outside of Project Euler.

When I came to college and discovered competitive programming, I tried out for the ACM ICPC team my very first quarter. After two hours of trying to complete the Kattis contest, though, I realised my knowledge of computer science had huge holes for the lack of any formal education in it, and decided to take try out again after taking CS 31.

A couple quarters later, even after I had taken CS 31 and 32, I hesitated to try out for ICPC for fear I wouldn’t do much better than I did in that first try-out, where I couldn’t solve a single problem and only in part because of trouble with I/O. I was also working 20 hours a week and taking 4-5 classes each quarter to make an early graduation possible. Not much has changed in that respect, except that entering my last year at UCLA, a year I’ll likely spend fully away from campus, has given me perspective on what’s important to me. I genuinely love solving computational problems, and I’d be remiss if I didn’t even try to involve myself in ICPC and learn from other people who are interested in the same thing and really good at it too. The workshops I’ve attended these past few weeks have also been nothing but encouraging; while I don’t know if I’ll get a chance to compete before I graduate, I do certainly want to work for ICPC and maybe help make that possible for other people.
column18:
The two-language problem! I fully admit recency bias is half the reason this is my answer, and the other half is that I’ve finally discovered (what I think is) a solution to this problem in the Julia programming language, but it’s something I’ve grappled with since I first started programming. 

Python was my first language of choice because I was then more interested in solving Project Euler problems than I was in learning programming, and it took minimal effort to pick up and work with. As I started doing some of the more challenging problems though, I found myself woefully limited by how slow it was and reluctant to pick up a faster language that would increase my prototyping time. After learning C++ in CS 31 and CS 32 (and foundational computer science, of course), I started solving Project Euler problems in the much faster language but quite missed using Python for how much easier it used to be to right code in. After a little bit of research over the summer about this problem, I learned it’s a common version of what’s called the two-language problem, and discovered Julia, a supposed solution to it — a programming language as high-level as Python and far closer to C/C++ in performance.

I’ve been programming in Julia for the past couple of months now, and learning the language (and its own shortcomings) has helped develop special appreciation for the two-language problem. Through the Julia community, I’ve learned about and grown more interested in the more technical aspects of writing performant code, and now keenly follow how different programming paradigms grow to address the two-language problem.
